cards.question: What is an operating system and what are its main functions?
core: A set of programs that sits between applications software and the computer hardware and manages the computer's resources.
Three main functions: manage the computer's Resources (CPU, memory, devices), establish a user Interface, and execute and provide services for Applications software.
The lowest level of every OS is the kernel - the first layer loaded into memory when the system boots.
cards.mnemonic: R-I-A: Resources, Interface, Applications.
more: Key tasks: processor management, memory management, device management (drivers translate signals between the OS and hardware), application interface (APIs), user interface.
Four general types: real-time (RTOS, controls machinery), single-user single-task (PDAs), single-user multi-tasking (most PCs), multi-user multi-tasking.
Partial-credit examples of operating systems: Windows, Linux, macOS, FreeBSD, Android, iOS.

cards.question: Compare Windows and Linux.
core: Windows is proprietary, closed-source, paid software developed by Microsoft; first released on November 20, 1985; holds about 80% of the desktop market.
Linux is free, open-source software developed by a community (Linus Torvalds and the GNU Project); its source code is freely available; it dominates servers and supercomputers.
Windows offers a GUI with limited customization and grew out of MS-DOS; Linux offers both GUI (KDE, Gnome) and command line, is highly customizable, and is based on Unix.
cards.mnemonic: Windows: closed, paid, desktops. Linux: open, free, servers.
more: Dates: Windows first release 1985; Linux kernel 1991, first distribution 1992.
Fun fact: the Linux mascot is Tux the penguin, and effectively all of the world's top supercomputers run Linux.

cards.question: Tell the short history of Linux.
core: 1983: Richard Stallman announces the GNU Project (Free Software Foundation) - a fully free operating system; development starts in January 1984.
1991: Linus Torvalds, a computer science student in Helsinki, annoyed by the shortcomings of Minix, writes his own kernel.
1992: the GNU system's missing piece - a kernel - is filled by Linux, and the first Linux distribution appears.
cards.mnemonic: '83 announced, '84 started, '91 kernel, '92 distro - GNU waited eight years for its kernel.
more: A kernel is the core of an OS: it handles memory allocation, talks to hardware devices and keeps everything running.
Today Linux powers phones, tablets, routers, game consoles, desktops, mainframes and supercomputers; the Linux Foundation is the non-profit fostering its growth.
If years slip your mind, the safe story: a Finnish student wrote the kernel as a hobby project in the early 90s, the free-software GNU Project supplied the rest, and the combination became GNU/Linux.

cards.question: What is a Linux distribution?
core: A distribution is a complete Linux operating system kit: the kernel + a GUI (such as KDE or Gnome) + utilities (many supplied by the GNU Project) + a large collection of applications (word processors, spreadsheets, media players, databases).
Popular mainstream distributions: Debian (and derivatives such as Ubuntu), Fedora, openSUSE.
cards.mnemonic: Distro = kernel + GUI + utilities + apps.
more: Distributions exist because Linux by itself is only a kernel - someone has to package everything else around it.
X is the distributed windowing system on which graphical interfaces for Linux (KDE, Gnome) are implemented.

cards.question: What is the Free Software Foundation?
core: An organization of volunteers founded by Richard Stallman in 1983.
Its ideal: making good software that anyone can use without paying.
It initiated development of the GNU operating system in January 1984; the goal of a fully free OS was achieved in 1992, when Linus Torvalds' Linux kernel filled the last gap.
cards.mnemonic: GNU = "GNU's Not Unix" - a recursive acronym.
more: GNU supplied the compiler, utilities and tools; Linux supplied the kernel - together they form GNU/Linux.
Even if you forget the dates, remember the pairing: Stallman = ideology and GNU; Torvalds = the kernel.

cards.question: Who is Richard Stallman?
core: The founder of the Free Software Foundation and initiator of the GNU Project (announced in 1983).
He believed software should be freely available for anyone to use, modify and share.
His work led to the GNU/Linux operating system ecosystem and the open-source movement as we know it.
more: Anchor for the exam: Stallman and Torvalds are two different people - Stallman started the free-software movement and GNU, Torvalds wrote the Linux kernel eight years later.

cards.question: What is BSD?
core: BSD stands for Berkeley Software Distribution.
It is a Unix derivative distributed by the University of California, Berkeley, starting in the 1970s.
The name is also used collectively for its modern descendants - one of several branches of Unix operating systems.
cards.mnemonic: BSD = Berkeley + Unix + the 1970s.
more: Modern descendants worth naming: FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD.

cards.question: What is FreeBSD?
core: An advanced, free, open-source operating system for modern server, desktop and embedded platforms, with a code base under continuous development for decades.
It is developed as a complete operating system: the kernel, device drivers and all utilities are held in the same source code tree.
It is regarded as very reliable and robust - the most common free OS on Netcraft's list of the 50 web servers with the longest uptime.
more: Features worth listing: preemptive multitasking, multi-user facilities, strong TCP/IP networking, memory protection, X Window System (GUI), thousands of ready-to-run applications, extensive documentation.
It can also run most Linux programs thanks to binary compatibility.

cards.question: What is the difference between Linux and FreeBSD?
core: Development model: FreeBSD is developed as a complete OS - kernel, drivers and utilities in one source tree; Linux is a kernel developed by one group, utilities by others (GNU), packaged into distributions by third groups.
Origin: Linux began as Linus Torvalds' student project (1991); FreeBSD descends from Berkeley Unix (1970s).
Licensing: Linux uses the GPL; BSD systems use the more permissive BSD license. FreeBSD can run most Linux applications via binary compatibility.
cards.mnemonic: FreeBSD = one tree, whole OS; Linux = kernel + kit of parts.
more: Both are considered very stable; FreeBSD's reputation comes from those record server uptimes (Netcraft list).
BSD variants to mention: FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD; Linux distributions: Ubuntu, Fedora, openSUSE.

cards.question: What is the binary compatibility between FreeBSD and Linux?
core: Binary compatibility means FreeBSD can run most programs built for Linux without modification.
It runs them as well as - and sometimes even better than - Linux itself; the classic example is running the Linux version of Oracle on FreeBSD.
This makes FreeBSD very flexible: users get the large Linux software ecosystem without running Linux natively.
more: If the term slips your mind, describe it in plain words: FreeBSD can run Linux software directly, so you don't have to choose between the two ecosystems.

cards.question: What is Mac OS X and what does the X mean?
core: Mac OS is the trademarked name for Apple's series of graphical operating systems for Macintosh computers; it is credited with popularizing the graphical user interface, introduced with the first Macintosh in 1984.
The X is the Roman numeral 10, officially pronounced "ten" - it continues the numbering after Mac OS 8 and 9 (though people often say the letter X).
There are two families: the "classic" Mac OS (from 1984, fully graphical, no command line) and OS X, which is Unix-based and was renamed macOS in 2016.
cards.mnemonic: X = ten (Roman numeral), not the letter.
more: Deeper detail if you remember it: OS X is built on NeXTSTEP and the Mach kernel, and it is the basis for iOS on iPhones and iPads.
Fun fact: Apple introduced the Macintosh with the famous "1984" commercial aired during the Super Bowl.

cards.question: What is a computer network?
core: A number of computers and peripherals linked together so they can share resources and communicate.
Networks allow sharing of data and files, sharing of peripherals (printers, scanners), communication between users, and access to shared processing power.
more: Key components to name for extra points: file servers, bridges, routers, gateways, backbones, LANs, modems, hubs.

cards.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of computer networks?
core: Advantages: sharing resources (files, printers, internet connection), communication between users (email, chat), centralized data storage and management, cost savings through shared hardware, easier collaboration.

Disadvantages: security risks (unauthorized access, viruses), dependence on infrastructure (if the server goes down, clients cannot work), installation and maintenance costs, risk of data loss or corruption, traffic congestion with many simultaneous users.
cards.mnemonic: Advantages = sharing + communication; disadvantages = security + dependence + cost.
more: A strong closing sentence: most disadvantages (security, congestion, server dependence) can be managed with firewalls, backups and good administration, so the benefits usually outweigh them.

cards.question: What is network topology?
core: The physical or logical arrangement of computers and devices in a network - how they are connected and how data flows between them.
more: Physical topology = how the cables are laid out; logical topology = how the data actually travels.
Be ready to name the common ones: bus, star, ring, mesh.

cards.question: Briefly explain the most common network topologies.
core: Bus: all computers on one main cable - simple and cheap, but if the cable fails the entire network goes down.
Star: all computers connect to a central hub or switch - one failed cable affects one device, but a failed hub takes down the whole network.
Ring: each computer connects to exactly two others, forming a loop; data travels in one direction; a failure at any point can disrupt the entire network.
Mesh: every device connects to every other device - very reliable with no single point of failure, but expensive and complex.
cards.mnemonic: Bus = one cable, star = one hub, ring = one loop, mesh = everything to everything.
more: Choosing between them is a cost-versus-reliability trade-off: bus is the cheapest, mesh is the most reliable, and star is the practical middle ground used in most modern LANs.

cards.question: Describe the bus topology, with advantages and disadvantages.
core: All computers and devices connect to one central cable - the bus or backbone. Data travels along the cable and every device receives each signal, but only the intended recipient processes it.
Advantages: simple and cheap to set up, needs the least cabling, easy to extend.
Disadvantages: if the main cable fails, the entire network goes down; performance degrades as devices are added; difficult to troubleshoot; not suitable for large networks.
cards.mnemonic: One cable = one point of failure.
more: Historical note for context: early Ethernet networks really were a bus on coaxial cable, before star wiring with hubs and switches took over.

cards.question: Describe the mesh topology.
core: Every device is connected directly to every other device in the network.
Two types: full mesh (every node to every other node) and partial mesh (some nodes fully interconnected, others connected only to the nodes they exchange the most data with).

Advantages: very reliable and fault-tolerant - if one link fails, data reroutes through another path; no single point of failure; dedicated links give high performance. Disadvantages: very expensive (a lot of cabling), complex to set up and manage, impractical for large networks.
cards.mnemonic: Mesh = maximum reliability for maximum cost.
more: Real-world example: the Internet backbone itself is a partial mesh - multiple routes exist between major nodes, so traffic reroutes around failures.

cards.question: Describe the star topology, with advantages and disadvantages.
core: All devices connect to a central hub or switch.
Advantages: easy to add or remove devices without affecting the rest; a failed cable or device affects only that device; easy to troubleshoot and manage; better performance than bus.
Disadvantages: if the central hub or switch fails, the entire network goes down (single point of failure); needs more cabling than bus; the hub adds cost.
cards.mnemonic: Hub down = network down.
more: Star is the most common topology in modern LANs - a home Wi-Fi network is logically a star with the router in the middle.

cards.question: What is a PAN (Personal Area Network)?
core: A network used for communication among devices close to one person - its reach is typically a few meters.
It connects personal devices to each other (intrapersonal communication) or links them to a higher-level network and the Internet.
PANs can be wired (USB, FireWire) or wireless (Bluetooth, IrDA) - the wireless kind is called a WPAN.
cards.mnemonic: PAN = one Person, a few meters.
more: Everyday example to offer: your phone, smartwatch and wireless earbuds connected over Bluetooth form a PAN.

cards.question: What is a LAN (Local Area Network)?
core: A network covering a small geographic area such as a home, an office, or a group of buildings.
Each computer has its own computing power but can also access other devices on the network, subject to permissions.
Three defining characteristics: much higher data transfer rates than WANs, smaller geographic range, and no need for leased telecommunication lines.
more: The first LANs were created in the late 1970s.

cards.question: What is an intranet?
core: A private network limited in scope to a single organization or entity.
It uses the same technology as the Internet - the TCP/IP protocol suite, HTTP, FTP - but is not publicly accessible.
Depending on its size, an intranet may be categorized as a LAN, CAN, MAN or WAN.
cards.mnemonic: Intranet = Internet technology, internal use only.
more: Everyday example: a company portal with documents and internal tools that employees can open, but nobody outside the company can reach.

cards.question: What is an extranet?
core: A private network that uses Internet technology and the public telecommunication system to securely share part of a business's information or operations with suppliers, vendors, partners and customers.
It can be viewed as the part of a company's intranet that has been extended to users outside the company.
It requires security and privacy: firewall server management, digital certificates, user authentication, message encryption, VPNs (Virtual Private Networks).
more: Example to offer: a supplier logging into a company's ordering system - an outsider working securely inside one part of the company network.

cards.question: What is the difference between an intranet and an extranet?
core: Intranet: internal users only (employees), for internal communication and resource sharing, within a single organization.
Extranet: access extended to external partners, customers and vendors, for collaboration across organizations.
Because outsiders connect over public infrastructure, the extranet needs stronger security: VPNs, encryption, certificates, authentication.
cards.mnemonic: Intra = inside the company; extra = extended outside.
more: One-line summary that earns points: an extranet is an intranet extended to trusted outsiders.

cards.question: What is the World Wide Web?
core: A network of documents that works in a hypertext environment - text that contains links (hyperlinks) to other documents.
Web pages are stored on computers acting as servers; your computer is the client and uses a web browser to access and download them.
Pages are organized into websites, maintained by a webmaster; the Web carries interactive multimedia information and has become a real information highway.
more: Partial-credit fact: the Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN around 1990.
Key vocabulary to drop in: hypertext, hyperlink, client, server, browser, website, webmaster.

cards.question: What is the difference between the WWW and the Internet?
core: The Internet is the global network infrastructure - the physical network connecting millions of computers using TCP/IP.
The WWW is a service that runs on top of the Internet: a collection of hyperlinked documents accessed with a browser over HTTP/HTTPS.
The Internet is broader - it also carries email, FTP, VoIP, online gaming - while the Web is specifically web pages and hyperlinks.
cards.mnemonic: Internet = infrastructure (TCP/IP); Web = one service on it (HTTP).
more: One-sentence version for the exam: the Internet is the network; the Web is one of the services that uses it.

cards.question: What is an IP address?
core: A unique numerical label assigned to each device connected to a computer network.
It serves two main functions: identifying the host and providing the location of that host in the network.
A DNS server converts human-readable domain names (www.google.com) into IP addresses (like 205.136.145.101) so browsers can find the correct web server.
cards.mnemonic: IP = who you are + where you are on the network.
more: Versions worth mentioning: IPv4 (numbers like 192.168.0.1) and the newer IPv6, introduced because IPv4 addresses were running out.

cards.question: What is TCP?
core: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is one of the core communication protocols of the Internet protocol suite.
It provides reliable, ordered and error-checked delivery of data between applications communicating over an IP network.
It is used for web browsing (HTTP/HTTPS), email and file transfers.
cards.mnemonic: TCP = packets arrive, correct and in order.
more: The suite is usually named TCP/IP: IP finds the way to the destination, TCP makes sure the data arrives intact.

cards.question: What is DNS?
core: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (www.fit.ba) into the IP addresses (205.136.145.101) that computers use to identify each other.
When you type a URL, the browser first contacts a DNS server to get the IP address, then connects to the web server at that address.
more: DNS is often described as the phonebook of the Internet: you look up a name and get back a number.
Without DNS the Web would still work - but you would have to memorize numeric addresses for every site.

cards.question: What is a router and what is its function?
core: A special computer that directs communication messages when several networks are connected together.
It examines the destination IP address of each data packet and forwards it toward its destination along the most efficient path.
High-speed routers can serve as part of the Internet backbone; at each end of a WAN leased line, a router connects the LAN on one side and a hub within the WAN on the other.
more: Relatable example: your home "router" does several jobs at once - routing, Wi-Fi access point, and usually the gateway to your Internet provider.

cards.question: What is a gateway?
core: An interface that enables dissimilar networks to communicate - for example, two LANs based on different topologies or different network operating systems.
It acts as a "door" between two different types of networks, translating between them so data can pass through.
In everyday use it also names the device (often a router) that gives a local network access to the Internet.
more: In practice your home router acts as the default gateway - every packet leaving your network passes through it.

cards.question: What is a URL?
core: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the address of a resource on the World Wide Web - it tells the browser exactly where to find a web page.

Example breakdown of http://www.hw.ac.uk/libWWW/irn/irn.html: protocol prefix (http://), web service indicator (www), domain name (hw.ac), country code (uk), directory path (libWWW/irn/), document name (irn.html).
cards.mnemonic: Protocol + www + domain + country + path + document.
more: If you can't recall the sample URL, invent a simple one and label its parts - the labels carry the points.

cards.question: Explain gateway and URL and how they work together.
core: A gateway is the interface that enables dissimilar networks to communicate - the device that lets your local network connect to the Internet.
A URL is the web address you type into the browser to reach a specific resource on the Web.

Together: when you type a URL, the request travels through your network's gateway (usually a router) out onto the Internet and reaches the web server hosting the resource - the gateway handles the routing out, the URL specifies the exact destination.
cards.mnemonic: Gateway = the way out of your network; URL = the destination address.
more: If asked separately, keep the split clear: a gateway is a network device, a URL is a text address - they meet only in the browsing process.

cards.question: What is a bridge?
core: A hardware and software combination used to connect networks of the same type.
It can also partition a large network into two smaller ones, or connect two LANs that are near each other.
Unlike a gateway, a bridge connects networks of the same type - not dissimilar ones.
more: Why partition a network with a bridge: splitting a large network into two smaller ones reduces the traffic on each half.

cards.question: What is the difference between a bridge, a gateway and a router?
core: A bridge connects networks of the same type, and can also partition a large network into two smaller ones.
A gateway is an interface that enables dissimilar networks to communicate (different topologies or network operating systems).
A router directs communication messages between connected networks: it reads each packet's destination IP address and forwards it along the most efficient path.
cards.mnemonic: Bridge = same + same; gateway = different + different; router = reads IPs, picks the path.
more: All three connect networks - what differs is what they connect and how smart they are: bridge (same type), gateway (different types), router (chooses paths by IP address).

cards.question: What is a peer-to-peer network?
core: A distributed system in which all component nodes participate in similar roles - they are peers to each other.
There is no central server: each node can act as both client and server, finds its peers, and helps maintain the system structure.
It is a decentralized network architecture.
more: Partial-credit examples of P2P in practice: file sharing (BitTorrent) and cryptocurrency networks.

cards.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of a peer-to-peer network?
core: Advantages: all clients contribute resources (bandwidth, storage, computing power), so total capacity grows as nodes join (scalability); data replicated over multiple peers makes it fault-tolerant with no single point of failure; no expensive dedicated servers.

Disadvantages: nodes are more complex (each must find peers and maintain the system); security is harder to control without a central authority; performance can vary with what individual peers provide.
cards.mnemonic: More peers = more capacity; no boss = harder security.
more: Known example for extra points: BitTorrent file sharing - each downloader also uploads, which is exactly why capacity grows as more peers join.

cards.question: Compare peer-to-peer and client-server systems.
core: P2P is decentralized: every node is both client and server and contributes resources - so more nodes means more total capacity, and there is no single point of failure.

Client-server is centralized: servers provide the resources and control access - better security and easier management, but the server is a single point of failure and congests when many clients send requests at once.
In one line: P2P scales and survives failures; client-server is easier to secure and manage.
more: Complexity comparison: P2P has complex nodes and simple infrastructure; client-server has simple clients and a complex, expensive server.

cards.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of a client-server system?
core: Advantages: better security - data is stored on servers with centralized access control; the server controls who can access and change data; centralized data management and backups; consistent performance for all clients.

Disadvantages: traffic congestion when many clients send requests simultaneously; single point of failure (if the server is down, client requests cannot be fulfilled); higher cost of server hardware and maintenance; less robust than P2P.
cards.mnemonic: Client-server wins on control, loses on the single point of failure.
more: Everyday examples: web servers, email servers, a school's information system - almost every website you use is client-server.

cards.question: What is a mobile network?
core: A mobile (cellular) network is a radio network distributed over land areas called "cells", each served by at least one base station.
It allows mobile devices (phones, tablets) to communicate and access the Internet without a fixed physical connection.
The technology generations are 2G, 3G, 4G (LTE) and 5G.
cards.mnemonic: Cells + base stations = cellular network.
more: Why "cells": coverage is divided into cell areas so the same radio frequencies can be reused in non-adjacent cells, which multiplies capacity.

cards.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of mobile networks?
core: Advantages: mobility (communicate from virtually anywhere), wide coverage across cities and countries, Internet and data services on the go, voice + SMS + data at the same time.

Disadvantages: coverage gaps in remote areas, security risks (wireless transmission can be intercepted), cost of data plans, speeds slower than wired connections in congested areas, and an ongoing debate about radiation and health.
more: What each generation added, in one line: 2G brought SMS and basic data, 3G mobile Internet, 4G video streaming, 5G high speed with low latency.

cards.question: What is Wi-Fi?
core: A wireless networking technology that uses radio waves to provide high-speed Internet and network connections.
It allows devices to connect to a local area network (LAN) without physical cables.
Wi-Fi is based on the IEEE 802.11 standards and is the most common wireless connectivity in homes, offices and public spaces.
cards.mnemonic: Wi-Fi = wireless LAN, standard IEEE 802.11.
more: "Wi-Fi" is a brand name certified by the Wi-Fi Alliance - devices carrying the logo are tested to work together.

cards.question: What are wireless connections? Give examples of wireless services.
core: Wireless connections let devices communicate without physical cables, using radio waves, infrared or microwave signals.

Examples: Wi-Fi (wireless LAN in homes, offices, cafes), Bluetooth (short-range - headphones, keyboards; used in PANs), mobile data 2G/3G/4G/5G (cellular Internet), GPS (satellite positioning), satellite Internet (remote areas), IrDA (infrared, line-of-sight, e.g. TV remotes), WiMAX (wide-area wireless broadband).
more: For each example be ready to add one use: Wi-Fi at home and in cafes, Bluetooth for accessories, GPS for navigation, satellite Internet where cables don't reach.
Fun fact: Bluetooth is named after the 10th-century Scandinavian king Harald Bluetooth.

cards.question: What is GPS?
core: GPS (Global Positioning System) is a satellite-based navigation system that provides location and time information to a GPS receiver anywhere on Earth.
It requires an unobstructed line of sight to four or more GPS satellites.
Uses: navigation (car GPS, phone maps), surveying, aviation, military applications, location-based services.
cards.mnemonic: Four satellites in sight = a fix on your position.
more: Why four satellites: three are enough to compute a position, and the fourth corrects the receiver's clock.

cards.question: What is cloud computing?
core: The delivery of computing services over the Internet ("the cloud"): servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics.
Instead of owning their own infrastructure, organizations rent access to computing resources from a cloud provider, typically pay-as-you-go.
more: Partial-credit examples: Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive as everyday cloud storage; AWS and Microsoft Azure as cloud platforms.

cards.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of cloud computing?
core: Advantages: cost savings (no own servers to buy or maintain), scalability (resources up or down as needed), accessibility from anywhere, automatic updates by the provider, easier disaster recovery and backups, collaboration on the same files.

Disadvantages: dependence on the Internet (no connection = no access), security and privacy risks (data on third-party servers), less control over data and infrastructure, vendor lock-in (switching providers is hard), subscription costs add up, provider downtime blocks your access.
cards.mnemonic: The cloud trades control for convenience.
more: Terms that impress if you remember them: public, private and hybrid cloud; service models IaaS, PaaS and SaaS.

cards.question: What are the main email protocols?
core: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) - for sending email, from client to server and between servers.
POP3 (Post Office Protocol 3) - for receiving: downloads messages to one device and typically deletes them from the server.
IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) - for receiving: messages stay on the server, so several devices see the same mailbox.
cards.mnemonic: SMTP Sends; POP Pulls off the post; IMAP stays In place.
more: One practical sentence for extra points: modern mail apps use IMAP because people read mail on both phone and laptop.

cards.question: What makes a good website?
core: Good design: consistent layout, menus in the same place on every page, consistent colors and fonts, a clearly visible logo.
Easy navigation, ease of use, and fast loading - pages should not be too complex or slow.
Accurate and up-to-date content, helpful graphics that support the content, compatibility across browsers and devices, and visible contact information.
cards.mnemonic: Design, navigation, accuracy, freshness, speed, contact.
more: An easy structure for the written answer: design + navigation + content (accurate, updated) + technical (fast, compatible) + contact information.

cards.question: What are the features (basic elements) of a web page?
core: Web pages are created with HTML (HyperText Markup Language) and interpreted by a web browser.

Basic elements: text (various sizes, styles, fonts), links (hyperlinks to other pages or files), graphics (JPEG for photos with many colors, GIF for simpler graphics like buttons and icons), tables (for data or layout), frames (multiple HTML documents on one page), multimedia (animations, audio such as MIDI/WAV/MP3, video), and plug-ins (extra software for specific content).
cards.mnemonic: JPEG = photos with many colors; GIF = simple graphics and icons.
more: If the element list slips your mind, walk through any web page you know and name what you see: text, links, images, tables, video - that recall trick covers most of the answer.

cards.question: What are common mistakes made in the development of web pages?
core: Inappropriate domain name (too long, vague or unrelated to the business) and poor design or functionality (inconsistent menus, mixed link colors and fonts).
Too complicated or too slow (heavy animations that confuse casual users), a stagnant site that is never updated, broken links and 404 error messages.
No contact information, ignoring visitor statistics (missed chances to improve), and free or cheap hosting whose downtime damages credibility.
cards.mnemonic: Eight sins: bad name, bad design, slow, stale, broken, unreachable, unmeasured, cheap hosting.
more: Each mistake is the negation of a rule from "what makes a good website" - if you remember one list, you can derive the other.

cards.question: What happens in the background when we open a website?
core: You type the URL into the browser; the browser sends it to a DNS server, which translates the domain name into an IP address.
The request goes through your router/gateway and travels across the Internet, passing through various routers, until it reaches the remote web server.

The server looks for the requested document and sends the web page back to your browser (the client), which displays it on screen. Clicking a hyperlink repeats the process - after checking the cache first.
cards.mnemonic: URL, DNS, IP, routers, server, page, display.
more: Extra detail: you can bookmark a page to return to it easily, or use the Home button to go back to your start page.
Name the roles for extra points: your computer is the client, the DNS server resolves the name, routers forward packets, the web server hosts the page.

cards.question: Exam combo: explain TCP, DNS, and the advantages and disadvantages of the star topology.
core: TCP provides reliable, ordered and error-checked delivery of data packets across the Internet - data sent from one computer arrives correctly at the other.
DNS translates human-readable domain names (www.fit.ba) into the IP addresses computers use to locate each other.
Star topology: easy to add or remove devices, one failure affects only that device, easy to troubleshoot; but the central hub is a single point of failure and it needs more cabling and cost than bus.
more: This question just recombines three earlier answers - if it appears, answer each part separately and briefly.
